items.name: [object Object]
items.notes: Low feed input; requires good water exchange.

items.name: Carp
items.feedRequirements: Rice bran and oilcake mixtures; supplemental pellets optional
preventiveActions: Provide wintering ponds or deeper refuges
commonDiseases: Low dissolved oxygen in wintering ponds
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Requires wintering phase in cold climates; hardy omnivore for pond polyculture.

items.name: Catla
items.feedRequirements: Balanced pellets; bran and oilcake blends
items.southAsiaNotes: Surface feeder complementing Rohu and Mrigal in Indian major carp systems.

items.name: Grass Carp
items.stockingDensityPerHectare: Introduce based on weed biomass in ponds
items.southAsiaNotes: Provides weed control and additional biomass in composite carp culture.

items.name: Mrigal
items.southAsiaNotes: Bottom feeder that completes IMC polyculture alongside Rohu and Catla.

items.name: Pangasius Catfish
items.feedRequirements: High-protein pelleted feeds with strong aeration
preventiveActions: Maintain biosecurity and regular probiotic use
Ensure continuous aeration and water exchange
items.stockingDensityPerSqMeter: High density possible in intensive systems
items.southAsiaNotes: Requires intensive management with robust aeration infrastructure.

items.name: Pike-Perch
items.feedRequirements: High-quality pellets supplemented with forage fish
items.stockingNotes: Low to moderate densities in ponds or cages during warm season
items.russiaEuropeNotes: High-value predator requiring careful handling; sensitive to transport and stress.

items.name: Rohu
items.feedRequirements: Rice bran and oilcake mixture; supplement with pelleted feeds
items.southAsiaNotes: Performs best in polyculture with Catla and Mrigal; stock before monsoon.

items.name: Siberian Sturgeon
items.feedRequirements: High-protein sinking pellets; meticulous water quality management
preventiveActions: Implement biosecurity and regular health monitoring
Maintain optimal oxygen levels and clean systems
items.stockingNotes: Hatchery fingerlings stocked in intensive ponds or tanks
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Multi-year grow-out for meat and caviar; requires licensing and strict compliance.

items.name: Silver Carp
items.stockingDensityPerHectare: Stock as surface filter feeder in polyculture
items.southAsiaNotes: Improves plankton utilization with Rohu, Catla, and Mrigal.

items.name: Tilapia
items.feedRequirements: Feed 2–3% bodyweight per day using 25–30% protein pellets
preventiveActions: Perform partial harvests to manage biomass
items.fijiNotes: Well adapted to Fiji ponds; ensure aeration and regular water exchange.

items.name: Trout
items.feedRequirements: High-protein floating feeds; maintain high dissolved oxygen
preventiveActions: Maintain strict biosecurity and regular health checks
items.stockingNotes: Raceways, tanks, or cages in cold, oxygen-rich waters
commonDiseases: Off-flavor due to poor water quality
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Requires cold (<16°C), clear water; year-round production with tempered flows in Karelia and Scandinavia.

items.name: Begonia
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Chrysanthemum
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Cosmos
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Daffodil
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Dahlia
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Geranium
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Gladiolus
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Hibiscus
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Hyacinth
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Iris
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Lily
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Marigold
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Morning Glory
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Pansy
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Peony
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Petunia
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Rose
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Snapdragon
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Sunflower
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Tulip
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Zinnia
items.notes: Maintain consistent watering; partial sun.

items.name: Beef Cattle
items.feedRequirements: Pasture grazing with haylage and mineral blocks; finish on pasture or high-energy rations
preventiveActions: Rotate pastures and provide shelter during severe cold
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Steppe grazing systems common; spring calving aligns with pasture flush and winter housing.

items.name: Broiler Chicken
items.feedRequirements: Starter, grower and finisher feeds with clean water
items.fijiNotes: Fast turnover for Fiji smallholders; ensure biosecurity to reduce losses.
items.southAsiaNotes: Commonly raised in commercial sheds; enforce biosecurity to prevent CRD and coccidiosis.

items.name: Buffalo
items.feedRequirements: Green fodder plus concentrate; access to wallowing or sprinklers
preventiveActions: Conduct routine deworming and maintain hygiene
items.southAsiaNotes: Thrives in hot-humid tracts; high-fat milk preferred for dairy processing.

items.name: Dairy Cattle
items.feedRequirements: Rotational pasture or green fodder with concentrate and mineral mix
preventiveActions: Maintain milking hygiene and schedule deworming
items.fijiNotes: Requires consistent pasture rotation and mineral supplementation in Fiji.
items.southAsiaNotes: Heat-tolerant indigenous breeds (Gir, Sahiwal) perform well under hot climates.

items.name: Duck
items.feedRequirements: Balanced poultry mash supplemented with scavenged feed; ensure clean water
preventiveActions: Vaccinate and provide sanitary water bodies
Maintain litter hygiene and periodic deworming
items.southAsiaNotes: Suited to wetlands and backwaters; integrate with paddy for pest control.

items.name: Goat
items.feedRequirements: Grazing plus tree leaves and supplemental mineral lick
preventiveActions: Vaccinate against PPR as per regional program
items.southAsiaNotes: Adapted to dryland and backyard systems; ensure shelter during monsoon.

items.name: Layer Chicken
items.feedRequirements: Layer mash with 16–18% protein and high calcium for shell quality
preventiveActions: Monitor and manage external parasites in housing
items.southAsiaNotes: Temperature moderation inside sheds maintains egg production.

items.name: Pig
items.fijiNotes: Performs well in humid tropics; maintain housing hygiene to limit disease.
items.southAsiaNotes: Popular in North-East India and Bangladesh; maintain hygiene and vaccination schedules.

items.name: Sheep
items.feedRequirements: Grazing with supplemental fodder or concentrates in stall-fed systems
items.southAsiaNotes: Suited to arid and semi-arid belts; manage shade and water during peak heat.
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Romanov sheep are cold-hardy and prolific for fibre and meat in continental winters.

items.name: Sisal / agave fibers
items.scientificName: Agave sisalana and related Agave spp.
items.notes: Perennial rosette fiber crops for dry and tropical semi-arid production systems; managed for repeated leaf cutting rather than a single annual harvest.
items.plantingSeason: Establish offsets at the start of rainy periods; manage perennial stands for staggered cutting over multiple years
items.spacing: 1.2–2.0 m between plants depending on plantation system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Low to moderate nutrient demand; avoid chronic moisture stress during stand establishment
preventiveActions: Remove heavily infested rosettes and damaged leaves promptly
Maintain drainage and stagger harvests to preserve perennial stand health
items.maturityNotes: Sisal and related agave fibers commonly take about 2-4 years to first commercial cutting, followed by repeated harvest cycles. Dry and tropical semi-arid climates are preferred; cool climates are generally unsuitable.

items.name: Apples and pears
items.notes: Shared pome-fruit record for apple and pear crop planning; use this family entry when the grower wants orchard-style maturity guidance rather than a cultivar-specific tree reference.
items.plantingSeason: Dormant planting in cool-season orchard windows; harvest timing is usually tracked from bloom to fruit maturity rather than from tree establishment
items.spacing: 4–6 m between trees depending on rootstock and training system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate annual nutrient demand with attention to nitrogen balance, calcium, and boron where deficient
preventiveActions: Prune for airflow and light distribution
Remove dropped fruit and diseased wood after harvest
items.maturityNotes: Apples and pears are commonly tracked from bloom to harvest over about 120-180 days, with chilling accumulation, frost risk, and late-season weather strongly affecting practical picking dates.

items.name: Avocado
items.notes: Subtropical to warm-temperate orchard crop with long flowering-to-harvest windows that vary strongly by variety and climate.
items.plantingSeason: Plant at the start of the rainy season or in mild irrigated windows; harvest timing is usually measured from flowering rather than planting date
items.spacing: 6–9 m orchard spacing depending on vigor and pruning system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate demand with strong emphasis on drainage, calcium, and balanced nitrogen
preventiveActions: Maintain mulch without burying the trunk collar
Prune to reduce canopy humidity and remove diseased wood
items.maturityNotes: Avocado maturity often spans about 6-12 months from flowering, with oil accumulation, climate, and cultivar deciding when fruit is truly ready to pick.

items.name: Bananas and plantains
items.notes: Family-level record covering dessert bananas and plantains, including ratoon systems in warm humid or irrigated tropical production zones.
items.plantingSeason: Year-round with staggered planting where water is reliable; ratoon timing depends on mat health and sucker management
items.spacing: 1.8 x 1.8 m or wider depending on variety, wind exposure, and density target
items.fertilizerRequirements: High potassium demand with regular organic matter and split nitrogen applications
preventiveActions: Use clean planting material and rogue weak suckers early
Maintain drainage, sanitation, and wind support for heavy bunches
items.maturityNotes: Bananas and plantains commonly reach first harvest in about 9-14 months, with ratoon crops often moving faster after establishment and plantain types usually maturing later than dessert bananas.

items.name: Barley
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.
preventiveActions: Use crop rotation and seed treatment
Apply fungicide programs when disease thresholds are reached
items.plantingSeason: Spring sowing April–May; harvest August
items.fertilizerRequirements: Balanced NPK with sulfur addition for malting quality
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Hardy, short-season grain for feed and brewing across Russia, Ukraine, and Baltic states.

items.name: Beans
items.notes: Rotate with cereals to improve nitrogen balance.

items.name: Beets
items.notes: Avoid water logging to prevent rot.

items.name: Berries
items.scientificName: Vaccinium spp. / Fragaria spp. / Rubus spp.
items.notes: Family-level berry record for blueberry, raspberry, blackberry, and similar systems. Strawberry remains available as its own catalog entry while still sharing the same maturity framework.
items.plantingSeason: Plant in cool-season or mild windows; harvest timing is usually tracked from flowering or canopy establishment depending on the berry system
items.spacing: 0.3–3 m depending on whether the crop is strawberry, bush berry, or cane berry
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate demand with steady moisture and strong attention to pH for blueberries
preventiveActions: Prune and ventilate canopies to keep fruit dry
Remove overripe or rotted berries frequently during harvest
items.maturityNotes: Berry crops vary widely: strawberries can move quickly, while blueberries, raspberries, and blackberries more often follow 60-120 day flowering-to-harvest windows shaped by climate and pruning system.

items.name: Buckwheat
items.plantingSeason: Sow May–June; harvest August–September
items.fertilizerRequirements: Low input; prefers light, well-drained soils
preventiveActions: Time sowing to avoid late frosts during bloom
Encourage pollinator activity for better seed set
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Quick-maturing pseudo-cereal valued for honey and grain in Altai and Baltic regions.

items.name: Cabbage
items.plantingSeason: Transplant April–May; harvest July–October
items.fertilizerRequirements: Basal NPK with side-dressed nitrogen; lime acidic soils
preventiveActions: Apply lime to maintain pH above 6.5
Implement IPM and rotation to break pest cycles
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Late-storage cultivars for winter supplies; manage clubroot with liming and rotations.

items.name: Carrots
items.notes: Avoid water logging to prevent rot.
items.plantingSeason: Fiji: April–September; Russia/Europe: Sow April–May, harvest August–September
items.fertilizerRequirements: Poultry manure 10 t/ha; NPK 200 kg/ha
preventiveActions: Apply Mancozeb or Kocide as needed
items.fijiNotes: Requires loose soil for straight roots; ideal for Fiji's cool-season districts.
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Use Nantes/Flakkee types; protect against Alternaria and root fly with seed treatment and netting.

items.name: Cassava
items.notes: Avoid water logging to prevent rot.

items.name: Chickpeas
items.notes: Rotate with cereals to improve nitrogen balance.
preventiveActions: Cultivate resistant varieties and treat seed
Use pheromone traps and IPM for pod borer
items.plantingSeason: Rabi sowing (October–November)
items.fertilizerRequirements: Starter nitrogen, adequate phosphorus, and Rhizobium inoculation
items.southAsiaNotes: Requires cool, dry season; terminal drought reduces pod fill.

items.name: Cotton
items.notes: Warm-season lint crop with high pest pressure and strong dependence on variety choice, irrigation timing, and a reliable frost-free season.
items.plantingSeason: Plant after soils warm and frost risk passes; schedule around the full picking window in dry or tropical systems
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate to high nutrient demand; balance nitrogen to avoid rank vegetative growth and delayed boll opening
preventiveActions: Destroy volunteer cotton and post-harvest residues to break pest cycles
Use refuge planning, selective sprays, and timely picking to protect lint quality
items.maturityNotes: Cotton typically reaches first pick in about 150-190 days, with the full picking window extending longer depending on variety and season. Warm temperate systems must fit within the frost-free period.

items.name: Cowpeas
items.notes: Rotate with cereals to improve nitrogen balance.

items.name: Faba beans
items.notes: Rotate with cereals to improve nitrogen balance.

items.name: Flax (linen)
items.notes: Fiber flax record kept separate from flaxseed/linseed so linen systems can carry distinct pest and maturity guidance.
items.plantingSeason: Sow in cool-season spring windows and harvest before seed maturity if targeting fine linen fiber
items.spacing: Dense sowing for long, fine stems
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate fertility; avoid excess nitrogen that increases lodging and coarse stems
preventiveActions: Use long rotations and residue management to reduce disease carryover
Harvest on time to protect bast quality and reduce lodging losses
items.maturityNotes: Fiber flax commonly reaches harvest in about 85-115 days in temperate and continental climates. Heat stress or lodging can force earlier harvest and lower linen quality.

items.name: Ginger
items.notes: Avoid water logging to prevent rot.

items.name: Grapes
items.scientificName: Vitis vinifera and related Vitis spp.
items.notes: Shared grape record for table and wine-style systems, with maturity measured primarily from budbreak and flowering rather than from vine age.
items.plantingSeason: Establish vines in dormant or mild-season windows; annual harvest timing is usually tracked from budbreak to fruit maturity
items.spacing: 2–3 m between vines with trellis spacing adjusted to the training system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate demand; avoid excessive nitrogen that can worsen canopy disease pressure
preventiveActions: Open the canopy through pruning and bunch-zone leaf management
Remove diseased clusters and wood promptly after harvest
items.maturityNotes: Grapes are often harvested about 110-180 days after budbreak, with table vs wine targets, canopy microclimate, and late-season weather driving the final pick date.

items.name: Hemp
items.notes: Multi-purpose crop grown for fiber, hurd, and sometimes seed; harvest timing may also be constrained by local legal and market requirements.
items.plantingSeason: Sow once soils warm in spring; align variety with daylength and legal harvest constraints
items.spacing: Dense stands for fiber; wider rows for dual-purpose systems
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate fertility with balanced nitrogen to support stem growth without excessive lodging risk
preventiveActions: Use rotations and open-canopy management to limit humid-canopy disease
Harvest in line with both fiber maturity and local compliance thresholds
items.maturityNotes: Fiber hemp often reaches cut stage in about 90-120 days, while dual-purpose systems may run about 120-150 days. Temperate and continental regions are the main fit.

items.name: Jute
items.notes: Warm, humid bast fiber crop linked to monsoon rainfall and retting quality; harvest timing strongly affects both fiber fineness and yield.
items.plantingSeason: Sow into warm, moist soils ahead of the main rainy season and harvest before stems become too coarse
items.spacing: Dense broadcast or narrow-row stands for uniform stems
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate fertility with good moisture supply; avoid nutrient stress that reduces stem growth
preventiveActions: Rotate away from repeated bast fiber crops and remove diseased residues
Harvest on time to balance fiber fineness with biomass yield
items.maturityNotes: Jute commonly reaches fiber harvest in about 100-140 days in warm humid climates. Dry or cold regions are generally unsuitable without strong irrigation or protection.

items.name: Kenaf
items.notes: Fast-growing bast fiber crop used for pulp, composites, and fiber; planting date and rainfall pattern strongly influence stem height and harvest quality.
items.plantingSeason: Plant into warm soils at the start of the rainy season or with irrigation support in dry areas
items.spacing: Dense stands for bast fiber, slightly wider for dual-purpose systems
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate fertility; maintain steady moisture for rapid stem elongation
preventiveActions: Use crop rotation and clean residues to reduce wilt carryover
Harvest before stems become coarse or heavily stressed
items.maturityNotes: Kenaf typically reaches fiber harvest in about 110-150 days in tropical and warm temperate climates. Cool continental or high-mountain areas are usually marginal.

items.name: Lentils
items.notes: Rotate with cereals to improve nitrogen balance.

items.name: Maize
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.
preventiveActions: Deploy pheromone traps and IPM interventions
items.plantingSeason: Kharif and Rabi windows depending on agro-climatic zone
items.spacing: 60–75 cm rows x 20–25 cm between plants
items.fertilizerRequirements: Basal NPK with split nitrogen; ensure good drainage and weed control
items.southAsiaNotes: Avoid waterlogging; vigilant scouting for fall armyworm is essential.

items.name: Mangoes
items.notes: Tropical to subtropical orchard crop with maturity windows usually measured from flowering rather than planting age.
items.plantingSeason: Plant in warm moist establishment windows; annual harvest timing is usually tracked from flowering to fruit maturity
items.spacing: 8–10 m orchard spacing depending on vigor and pruning system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Annual organic matter plus balanced NPK, with micronutrient support where flowering or fruit set is weak
preventiveActions: Prune and sanitize the orchard after harvest
Use traps and fallen-fruit removal to break fruit-fly cycles
items.maturityNotes: Mango commonly reaches harvest about 3-5 months after flowering, with rainfall, heat, and alternate bearing shifting the exact harvest window.

items.name: Millet
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.

items.name: Mustard
items.plantingSeason: Rabi (October–November)
items.fertilizerRequirements: Balanced NPK with sulfur supplementation and micronutrients as needed
preventiveActions: Treat seed and maintain field sanitation
items.southAsiaNotes: Cool temperatures during flowering improve pod set and oil content.

items.name: Oats
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.
preventiveActions: Treat seed with fungicide before sowing
items.plantingSeason: Sow April–May; harvest August
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate nitrogen; add potassium on light soils
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Performs on acidic soils in Belarus and Baltic states; short growing season friendly.

items.name: Onions
items.plantingSeason: Kharif and Rabi cycles
items.fertilizerRequirements: Basal phosphorus and potassium; split nitrogen doses; avoid standing water
preventiveActions: Rotate fungicides and use sticky traps
Maintain field hygiene and irrigation discipline
items.southAsiaNotes: Cure bulbs thoroughly for storage and reduce post-harvest losses.

items.name: Citrus
items.notes: Family-level citrus record covering oranges, lemons, limes, grapefruits, and similar orchard crops with climate-sensitive flowering-to-harvest windows.
items.plantingSeason: Plant in mild orchard-establishment windows; harvest timing is usually measured from flowering and fruit set rather than from planting date
items.spacing: 4–7 m depending on species, rootstock, and canopy vigor
items.fertilizerRequirements: Steady NPK with attention to micronutrients such as zinc and boron where deficiency is common
preventiveActions: Prune for airflow and scout flush regularly
Control ants and remove dropped fruit to reduce pest carryover
items.maturityNotes: Citrus maturity is commonly measured in months from flowering, with many harvest windows spanning about 6-12 months depending on fruit type, variety, and heat or irrigation regime.

items.name: Papaya
items.notes: Fast-growing tropical fruit crop with repeated harvest once production begins, so maturity is usually framed as time to first harvest rather than a single-cut crop cycle.
items.plantingSeason: Plant in warm frost-free windows with reliable drainage; first harvest usually begins months later and then continues in flushes
items.spacing: 1.8–2.5 m depending on vigor and row system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Regular split feeding with emphasis on balanced nitrogen and potassium
preventiveActions: Rogue badly diseased plants early and keep rows clean
Avoid waterlogging and remove dropped fruit frequently
items.maturityNotes: Papaya often reaches first harvest in about 8-10 months from planting, then continues with staggered or repeated picking under warm tropical conditions.

items.name: Pearl millet
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.
preventiveActions: Adopt resistant hybrids and treat seed with fungicide
items.plantingSeason: Kharif (rainfed season)
items.fertilizerRequirements: Apply basal NPK; supplement micronutrients on deficient soils
items.southAsiaNotes: Performs well on marginal soils with limited rainfall.

items.name: Pears
items.scientificName: Pyrus communis and related Pyrus spp.
items.notes: Specific pear entry kept for growers who choose pear directly instead of the shared apples-and-pears family record.
items.plantingSeason: Dormant planting in cool orchard windows; annual harvest is more reliably tracked from bloom and fruit development than from planting date
items.spacing: 4–6 m depending on rootstock and training system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate annual nutrient demand with attention to calcium, boron, and balanced nitrogen
preventiveActions: Open the canopy with annual pruning
Remove dropped fruit and blighted wood promptly
items.maturityNotes: Pears commonly mature over about 135-185 days from bloom, with harvest often targeted slightly before full tree ripeness for storage and shipping quality.

items.name: Peas
items.notes: Rotate with cereals to improve nitrogen balance.

items.name: Peppers
items.plantingSeason: April–September
items.fertilizerRequirements: Poultry manure 10 t/ha; apply NPK (13-13-13) at 200 kg/ha
preventiveActions: Apply Benomyl, Kocide or Dimethoate as required
items.fijiNotes: Cool-season crop in Fiji highlands; ensure consistent soil moisture and pest monitoring.

items.name: Pigeon peas
items.notes: Rotate with cereals to improve nitrogen balance.

items.name: Pineapple
items.notes: Long-cycle tropical fruit crop where the first harvest often arrives well after a year, with follow-up ratoon cycles moving faster than the establishment crop.
items.plantingSeason: Plant in warm moist windows with clean suckers or slips; flowering induction and ratoon management strongly influence the harvest calendar
items.spacing: 25–35 cm in double or triple rows depending on production system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate to high potassium demand with steady moisture and careful nitrogen management
preventiveActions: Use clean planting material and manage ant trails
Keep residues and heavily infested crowns out of the field
items.maturityNotes: Pineapple commonly takes about 15-18 months to first harvest, while ratoon or follow-up crops often shorten after the planting has established.

items.name: Stone fruits
items.notes: Family-level record covering peaches, plums, apricots, and cherries, all of which rely on temperate orchard cycles with bloom-to-harvest timing.
items.plantingSeason: Dormant orchard planting in cool-season windows; annual crop timing is usually measured from bloom to harvest
items.spacing: 4–6 m depending on species, rootstock, and training system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate annual nutrient demand with careful nitrogen timing to avoid weak fruit finish
preventiveActions: Prune to improve airflow and remove diseased wood
Collect dropped fruit and thin crowded canopies early
items.maturityNotes: Stone fruits are commonly harvested about 90-140 days after bloom, with cherries and early apricots tending to mature ahead of peaches and later plums.

items.name: Potatoes
items.notes: Avoid water logging to prevent rot.
items.plantingSeason: South Asia: Rabi (plains) and summer (hills); Russia/Europe: Plant May, harvest Aug–Sep
items.fertilizerRequirements: Basal NPK with earthing up; treat seed tubers before planting
preventiveActions: Use certified seed and adopt protective sprays
Ensure irrigation scheduling to prevent stress
items.southAsiaNotes: Requires cool nights; irrigation critical during tuber bulking.
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Cool summer nights in Karelia and Baltic regions favor tuber bulking; manage Colorado beetle.

items.name: Radish
items.notes: Avoid water logging to prevent rot.

items.name: Rice
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.
preventiveActions: Plant resistant varieties and treat seed before sowing
Adopt integrated pest management (IPM) including pheromone traps and need-based sprays
items.plantingSeason: Kharif (June–October) and Rabi (November–February in South India)
items.spacing: 20 x 15 cm for transplanted puddled fields
items.fertilizerRequirements: Basal NPK with top-dress nitrogen; maintain puddled fields and standing water
items.southAsiaNotes: Monsoon-dependent production; ensure consistent water depth during vegetative growth.
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Storage cultivars cured well ensure winter supply in Belarus and Gomel regions.

items.name: Rye
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.
preventiveActions: Use clean, treated seed and rotate with non-host crops
Employ fungicides if disease pressure builds
items.plantingSeason: Sow September–October; harvest July
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate nitrogen; balance nutrients to prevent lodging
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Extremely frost tolerant; thrives on sandy soils with protective snow cover.

items.name: Sorghum
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.
preventiveActions: Sow early to escape pest pressure
items.plantingSeason: Kharif (rainfed) and Rabi (post-rain) depending on zone
items.fertilizerRequirements: Basal NPK; tolerant to moderate drought
items.southAsiaNotes: Key staple in semi-arid tropics; also used for fodder and silage.

items.name: Soybeans
items.notes: Rotate with cereals to improve nitrogen balance.
items.plantingSeason: Kharif sowing (June–July)
items.fertilizerRequirements: Inoculate seed with Rhizobium; ensure balanced phosphorus and potassium
items.southAsiaNotes: Waterlogging severely affects nodulation and yield; ensure drainage.

items.name: Strawberries
items.notes: Specific strawberry entry retained alongside the broader berries family because strawberry systems often behave differently from bush and cane berries in maturity timing and harvest cadence.
items.plantingSeason: Establish in cool-season or mild protected windows; first commercial harvest may begin quickly once flowering starts
items.spacing: 25–35 cm plants x 75–100 cm rows depending on matted-row or plasticulture system
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate feeding with steady potassium and careful nitrogen near fruiting
preventiveActions: Mulch and ventilate rows to keep fruit off wet soil
Pick ripe fruit frequently and remove diseased berries
items.maturityNotes: Strawberries often begin first harvest about 45-90 days after establishment or flowering, while day-neutral and protected systems can shorten the first-pick window.

items.name: Sugar beet
items.notes: Cool-season industrial sugar crop; best suited to temperate and continental production zones, with irrigation support in dry regions.
items.plantingSeason: Sow in cool-season spring or autumn windows; harvest 5-7 months later when roots and sugar content are fully developed
items.fertilizerRequirements: High NPK demand; supply boron on deficient soils
preventiveActions: Use boron sprays and improve drainage
items.maturityNotes: Temperate and continental sugar beet usually matures in about 140-200 days. Early hybrids finish sooner, while heat and water stress in dry regions can shorten leaf life and limit sugar accumulation.
items.coldNotes: Core crop in south/central Russia and Europe; irrigation boosts root yield.

items.name: Sugarcane
items.notes: Long-duration cane crop grown as plant cane or ratoon; best suited to tropical and subtropical systems with reliable rainfall or irrigation.
items.plantingSeason: February–April or September–October depending on region
items.spacing: Ridges 90–120 cm apart with 2–3 bud setts per furrow
items.fertilizerRequirements: High NPK doses supplemented with organic manures and trash mulching
preventiveActions: Use healthy, treated setts and rogue infected stools
Maintain trash mulching and integrated pest management
items.maturityNotes: Plant cane commonly matures in about 10-18 months, while ratoon crops often finish in 8-14 months. Irrigation timing strongly affects cane weight and sugar content in dry regions.
items.southAsiaNotes: Requires assured irrigation and high nutrient inputs for optimal cane yields.

items.name: Stevia
items.notes: This catalog entry currently stands in for stevia and related minor sweetener crops; monk fruit and similar niche sweeteners are temporarily mapped here until they receive dedicated records.
items.plantingSeason: Transplant after frost in warm temperate zones, or maintain year-round in frost-free tropical highlands with irrigation
items.fertilizerRequirements: Moderate fertility; avoid excess nitrogen that can reduce leaf sweetness concentration
preventiveActions: Use raised beds and good airflow to protect the crown
Harvest before heavy flowering for best leaf quality and stevioside concentration
items.maturityNotes: Stevia usually reaches a first leaf harvest in about 90-140 days, with later cuts every 45-70 days where regrowth is strong. Cooler or high-mountain climates often need a longer season or protected cultivation.

items.name: Sunflower
items.plantingSeason: Sow April; harvest September
items.fertilizerRequirements: Balanced NPK; avoid excessive nitrogen to reduce lodging
preventiveActions: Adopt tolerant hybrids and rotate away from susceptible crops
Apply fungicides when disease pressure builds
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Major oilseed crop across Ukraine and southern Russia; maintain wider rotations to curb Sclerotinia.

items.name: Sweet potatoes
items.notes: Avoid water logging to prevent rot.

items.name: Taro
items.notes: Avoid water logging to prevent rot.
items.plantingSeason: All year
items.fertilizerRequirements: Organic manure 10 t/ha
items.fijiNotes: Thrives in wet lowlands; essential staple known locally as Dalo.

items.name: Tomatoes
items.plantingSeason: Multiple windows, avoid peak heat or heavy rains
items.fertilizerRequirements: Basal NPK plus organic manures; mulching and fertigation recommended
preventiveActions: Stake and prune plants; use drip irrigation
Adopt IPM nets and targeted sprays
items.southAsiaNotes: Optimum at 20–25°C; staggered planting maintains continuous supply.

items.name: Triticale
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.

items.name: Vanilla
items.notes: Requires trellising and hand pollination; pods mature 8–9 months after pollination.
items.fijiNotes: Shade-loving vine; maintain humidity and provide regular pruning.

items.name: Wheat
items.notes: Provide balanced NPK fertilizer before planting.
preventiveActions: Seed dressing and timely fungicide/insecticide sprays
Maintain snow cover and stubble to protect winter stands
items.plantingSeason: South Asia: Rabi (November–April); Russia/Europe: Winter sow Sep–Oct, harvest July; spring types Apr–May
items.fertilizerRequirements: Basal NPK; top-dress nitrogen at tillering and booting stages; split N for winter wheat systems
items.southAsiaNotes: Requires cool, dry winters for proper grain filling across the Indo-Gangetic plains.
items.russiaEuropeNotes: Winter wheat sown in Krasnodar/Kuban benefits from snow cover; manage stripe rust and snow mold.
